past_medical_history: Type 2 diabetes mellitus diagnosed at age 28, managed with oral hypoglycemics
Hypothyroidism diagnosed at age 30, on levothyroxine replacement
Cholecystitis with recurrent right upper quadrant pain over the past year
Episode of transient bilateral lower extremity numbness 2 years ago lasting 5 days, attributed to lumbar radiculopathy by orthopedics, resolved without treatment
Intermittent blurry vision in the right eye 8 months ago lasting 3 days, evaluated by ophthalmology and attributed to dry eye syndrome
family_history: Mother age 60 with type 2 diabetes and hypertension
Father age 62 with coronary artery disease, had CABG at age 55
Maternal uncle with thyroid disease (type unspecified)
No known family history of neurological disease, multiple sclerosis, or autoimmune conditions

social_history.living_situation: Lives with husband and two children (ages 4 and 7) in an apartment

neurological_exam.mental_status: Glasgow Coma Scale 8/15 (E2V2M4). Eyes open to pain only. Incomprehensible sounds. Withdraws to pain. Not following commands. Unable to assess higher cortical functions due to decreased level of consciousness. Appears globally aphasic — no verbal output or evidence of comprehension.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils 3 mm bilaterally, reactive to light. Corneal reflexes present bilaterally. No spontaneous eye movements observed; oculocephalic reflex intact. Facial grimace symmetric to noxious stimulation. Gag reflex present. Unable to assess remaining cranial nerves due to altered consciousness.
neurological_exam.motor: Severe quadriparesis. Upper extremities: trace movement (1/5) in deltoids and biceps bilaterally, no distal hand movements. Lower extremities: no spontaneous or stimulus-evoked movement (0/5) bilaterally. Tone is flaccid in all extremities. No fasciculations observed.
neurological_exam.sensory: Withdraws inconsistently to deep pain in upper extremities. No withdrawal to pain in lower extremities. Detailed sensory examination not possible due to altered consciousness.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 1+ bilaterally, triceps 1+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 1+ bilaterally. Patellar reflexes absent bilaterally. Achilles reflexes absent bilaterally. Plantar responses extensor bilaterally (bilateral Babinski signs).
neurological_exam.coordination: Unable to assess due to quadriparesis and altered consciousness.
neurological_exam.gait: Unable to assess. Patient is bedbound.
neurological_exam.additional: Onset of neurological deficits noted approximately 6 hours after an otherwise uneventful exploratory laparotomy under general anesthesia performed for recurrent right upper quadrant abdominal pain. Patient was reportedly neurologically intact preoperatively. No prior history of seizures. Urgent neurology consultation requested by surgical team.

patient.chief_complaint: Acute quadriplegia, decreased consciousness, and aphasia discovered postoperatively in the recovery unit
patient.history_present_illness: Mrs. Priya Krishnamurthy is a 34-year-old right-handed woman who was transferred to the intensive care unit approximately 6 hours following an otherwise uneventful exploratory laparotomy performed under general anesthesia for evaluation of recurrent right upper quadrant abdominal pain suspected to be related to chronic cholecystitis. The surgical procedure lasted approximately 90 minutes and was reported as uncomplicated, with stable hemodynamics throughout and no episodes of hypotension or hypoxia noted on the anesthesia record. In the post-anesthesia care unit, the nursing staff noted that the patient was not recovering consciousness as expected. She was noted to have no purposeful movement of any extremity, was making only incomprehensible sounds, and was not following commands. The surgical team initially attributed the findings to prolonged effects of general anesthesia, but after 3 hours without improvement, a neurology consultation was requested. On evaluation by the neurology team, the patient was found to have a Glasgow Coma Scale of 8, profound quadriparesis with only trace movement in the upper extremities and no movement in the lower extremities, absent deep tendon reflexes in the lower extremities, and bilateral extensor plantar responses. She appeared globally aphasic. A review of her preoperative assessment confirmed that she was neurologically intact, conversant, and ambulatory independently prior to surgery. Her medical history is notable for type 2 diabetes managed with metformin and glibenclamide, and hypothyroidism treated with levothyroxine. A review of her medical records reveals a prior episode of bilateral lower extremity numbness approximately 2 years ago that lasted 5 days and was attributed to lumbar radiculopathy, and an episode of transient blurry vision in the right eye 8 months ago attributed to dry eye. Both episodes resolved spontaneously. The acute and severe nature of the presentation in the post-surgical setting raises immediate concern for a perioperative vascular event such as spinal cord infarction, cerebrovascular accident, or a structural lesion unmasked by the physiological stress of surgery.

findings.location: Left cerebellar peduncle extending into the left side of the medulla oblongata

signal_characteristics.DWI: Partial restricted diffusion at the leading edge
signal_characteristics.post_contrast: Incomplete ring enhancement (open ring sign) with peripheral enhancement

findings.mass_effect: Expansion of the left cerebellar peduncle with mild compression of the fourth ventricle. No significant midline shift. Early obstructive hydrocephalus not present.
findings.borders: Partially well-defined with some irregular margins

findings.location: Right temporal lobe, involving both cortical gray matter and adjacent subcortical white matter
findings.mass_effect: Minimal local mass effect. No midline shift.

findings.location: Right periventricular white matter, adjacent to the frontal horn of the lateral ventricle

additional_observations: Major intracranial arteries demonstrate normal flow voids
Ventricular system is normal in size and configuration
Cervical spine not included in this study
mri.impression: Large expansile lesion centered in the left cerebellar peduncle extending into the left medulla oblongata, measuring approximately 3.8 cm, with peripheral (incomplete ring) enhancement and surrounding edema. A second T2/FLAIR hyperintense lesion in the right temporal lobe with faint peripheral enhancement. An additional small non-enhancing periventricular white matter lesion is noted. The dominant posterior fossa lesion has mass effect on the fourth ventricle but without hydrocephalus. Differential diagnosis includes high-grade glioma (e.g., glioblastoma), lymphoma, brain abscess, and tumefactive demyelination. The incomplete ring enhancement pattern may favor demyelination, but neoplasm cannot be excluded. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings: Sinus tachycardia, likely physiological in the postoperative setting
No ST-segment changes or T-wave inversions
No evidence of atrial fibrillation or other arrhythmia
ecg.interpretation: Sinus tachycardia at 102 bpm. Otherwise normal ECG. No evidence of acute cardiac ischemia or arrhythmia. Tachycardia likely related to postoperative physiological stress.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: WBC 11.2 x10^9/L (H), Neutrophils 78 % (H), Glucose 188 mg/dL (H), CRP 12.4 mg/L (H), HbA1c 7.8 % (H).

csf.glucose_ratio: 0.65 (serum glucose 110 mg/dL at time of LP; normal ratio)

special_tests.Oligoclonal bands: Not detected in CSF (0 bands unique to CSF)

csf.interpretation: Opening pressure: 16 cm. WBC: 3 cells/mm3 (normal) (N/A PMN/N/A lymph). Protein: 38 mg/dL. Glucose: 72 mg/dL (ratio 0.65 (serum glucose 110 mg/dL at time of LP; normal ratio)). Oligoclonal bands: Not detected in CSF (0 bands unique to CSF). IgG index: 0.58 (normal: <0.7). CSF cytology: No malignant cells identified. Flow cytometry: No monoclonal B-cell population identified. Gram stain: No organisms seen. CSF culture: No growth at 48 hours. AFB stain: Negative. Cryptococcal antigen: Negative. VDRL: Non-reactive.

findings.location: Cervical spinal cord at C3-C4 level, dorsolateral

additional_observations: No additional intramedullary lesions identified in the thoracic cord
No leptomeningeal enhancement along the spinal cord
Conus medullaris normal in position and signal
No evidence of longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis (lesion spans less than 3 vertebral segments)
output.impression: Short-segment T2 hyperintense lesion in the cervical spinal cord at C3-C4 level without enhancement. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings.signal: NAA_Cr_ratio: Reduced (1.2; normal >1.6) — consistent with neuronal injury or loss; Cho_Cr_ratio: Mildly elevated (1.4; normal <1.3) — suggests increased membrane turnover; Cho_NAA_ratio: Elevated (1.17) — borderline, not markedly elevated as expected in high-grade glioma (usually >2.0); Lactate: Small lactate doublet present — consistent with anaerobic metabolism in acute demyelination or tumor; Lipid: No significant lipid peak — argues against necrotic high-grade glioma; Glutamate_Glutamine: Mildly elevated — may be seen in active inflammatory processes; Myoinositol: Elevated — consistent with glial activation and inflammation, seen in both demyelination and low-grade glioma
findings.description: MR spectroscopy of left cerebellar peduncle lesion

output.impression: MR spectroscopy of the dominant left cerebellar peduncle lesion shows reduced NAA (neuronal injury), mildly elevated choline (increased membrane turnover), small lactate peak, no significant lipid, and elevated myoinositol. The absence of marked choline elevation and lack of lipid peak argue against GBM. However, spectroscopy alone cannot definitively exclude neoplasm, and clinical correlation is essential. Clinical correlation recommended.
Abnormal values: P100 latency (right eye) 118 ms ms (H).

output.query: tumefactive demyelination IV methylprednisolone pulse therapy response

results.title: Tumefactive demyelinating lesions: clinical features and outcomes with high-dose corticosteroids
results.summary: Retrospective study of 168 patients with biopsy-proven tumefactive demyelination. 70% showed significant clinical and radiographic improvement within 2-4 weeks of IV methylprednisolone pulse therapy (1g/day for 5 days). Early and dramatic response to steroids is a hallmark feature distinguishing tumefactive MS from CNS neoplasms.

results.title: Tumefactive multiple sclerosis: clinical and MRI features, treatment, and outcomes
results.summary: Review of tumefactive MS highlighting that open ring enhancement on MRI, response to corticosteroid pulse therapy, and young age of onset are key diagnostic features. Approximately 60-70% of tumefactive MS cases can be diagnosed without brain biopsy when clinical and radiological features are characteristic.

results.title: Distinguishing tumefactive demyelination from brain tumors: role of MR spectroscopy and corticosteroid response
results.summary: MR spectroscopy combined with clinical response to high-dose corticosteroids can differentiate tumefactive demyelination from neoplasm in most cases. Demyelinating lesions show moderate choline elevation (unlike marked elevation in glioma), elevated myoinositol, and reduced NAA, and typically show dramatic improvement after steroid therapy.

output.summary: Literature strongly supports the use of IV methylprednisolone pulse therapy (1g/day for 3-5 days) as both a diagnostic and therapeutic intervention in suspected tumefactive demyelination. A dramatic clinical and radiographic response to steroids is a hallmark feature that distinguishes tumefactive MS from CNS neoplasms. MR spectroscopy can provide supporting evidence. If no response to steroids is seen within 2-4 weeks, brain biopsy should be pursued.

findings.type: Interval decrease in size of demyelinating lesion
findings.location: Left cerebellar peduncle extending into the left medulla oblongata
findings.size: 2.4 x 1.8 x 1.5 cm (previously 3.8 x 2.5 x 2.2 cm)

signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: Hyperintense but reduced in extent compared to prior
signal_characteristics.DWI: No restricted diffusion (previously partial restriction noted)
signal_characteristics.post_contrast: Marked reduction in enhancement — only trace peripheral enhancement remains

findings.mass_effect: Significantly reduced. Fourth ventricle no longer compressed.
findings.borders: Better defined compared to prior study

findings.type: Interval decrease in T2/FLAIR hyperintense lesion

additional_observations: Dramatic interval improvement in both lesions following IV methylprednisolone pulse therapy
Periventricular lesion in right frontal white matter unchanged
output.impression: Dramatic interval improvement in the dominant left cerebellar peduncle lesion and the right temporal lobe lesion following 5-day course of IV methylprednisolone 1g/day. The dominant lesion has decreased from 3.8 cm to 2.4 cm with near-complete resolution of enhancement. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.proposed: IV Methylprednisolone 1000 mg daily for 5 days
interactions: Metformin + Methylprednisolone: Corticosteroids can cause significant hyperglycemia and may counteract the glucose-lowering effect of metformin. Close blood glucose monitoring required. May need to temporarily switch to insulin.

Glibenclamide + Methylprednisolone: Corticosteroids antagonize the hypoglycemic effect of sulfonylureas. Risk of severe hyperglycemia. Consider temporarily discontinuing glibenclamide and initiating insulin sliding scale.
warnings: Patient has diabetes mellitus — high-dose corticosteroids will significantly worsen glycemic control. Recommend insulin sliding scale during pulse therapy.
Monitor blood glucose at least 4 times daily during steroid therapy
GI prophylaxis with pantoprazole (patient already on this medication)
Monitor for steroid-related psychiatric side effects given ICU setting
alternatives: Plasma exchange (PLEX) if steroid contraindicated or inadequate response — 5-7 exchanges over 10-14 days
IV immunoglobulin (IVIg) 0.4 g/kg/day for 5 days as second-line alternative

output.query: tumefactive multiple sclerosis perioperative presentation unmasking

results.title: Tumefactive demyelination presenting as acute neurological deterioration after surgery
results.summary: Case series of 5 patients with previously undiagnosed demyelinating disease who presented with acute neurological deterioration in the perioperative period. General anesthesia and surgical stress may unmask or exacerbate subclinical CNS demyelinating disease. The physiological stress of surgery may trigger an acute MS relapse.

results.title: Tumefactive MS mimicking brain tumor: diagnostic challenges and therapeutic approaches
results.summary: Review of 26 cases of tumefactive MS initially misdiagnosed as brain tumors. Key distinguishing features include: open ring enhancement (vs. closed ring in GBM), younger patient age, multifocal lesions, negative CSF cytology, and dramatic corticosteroid response. Up to 40% of tumefactive MS cases initially undergo unnecessary brain biopsy.

output.summary: Tumefactive MS can present acutely in the perioperative period and frequently mimics brain tumors on initial imaging. Surgical stress and general anesthesia are recognized triggers for acute MS relapses. Key diagnostic features that distinguish tumefactive MS from neoplasm include open ring enhancement, MR spectroscopy pattern, negative CSF cytology, and dramatic response to corticosteroid pulse therapy.

differential.key_distinguishing: Ring-enhancing mass with mass effect mimics GBM, but open ring enhancement pattern, absence of lipid peak on MRS, negative CSF cytology, and dramatic steroid response favor tumefactive MS. GBM typically shows marked choline elevation and lipid/necrosis.

Can present with deep periventricular enhancing lesions and may transiently respond to steroids. Distinguished by typically homogeneous enhancement (not ring), positive CSF flow cytometry, restricted diffusion, and high choline on MRS.

Ring enhancement and DWI restriction can mimic abscess. Distinguished by absence of fever, no infectious source, negative CSF cultures and Gram stain, and normal inflammatory markers (no leukocytosis beyond postoperative stress).
Negative aquaporin-4 antibodies. Spinal cord lesion is short-segment (not longitudinally extensive). Brain lesions are atypical for NMOSD pattern.
Ring-enhancing lesions can mimic tuberculoma, especially in endemic regions. Excluded by negative AFB stain, normal CSF parameters, and dramatic steroid response.
Can cause enhancing CNS lesions. Excluded by normal ACE level, absence of leptomeningeal enhancement, negative ANA/ANCA, and no systemic sarcoidosis features.

optimal_actions.action: Obtain urgent brain MRI with gadolinium contrast to characterize the lesion(s) causing acute neurological deficits
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Large tumefactive lesion in left cerebellar peduncle/medulla with open ring enhancement, right temporal lobe lesion, and periventricular white matter lesion

optimal_actions.action: Order comprehensive laboratory workup including CBC, BMP, coagulation, inflammatory markers, thyroid function, autoimmune screen, and vitamin B12
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Labs largely normal except postoperative stress changes and hyperglycemia from diabetes. Negative autoimmune serologies.

optimal_actions.action: Perform lumbar puncture for CSF analysis including cytology, flow cytometry, oligoclonal bands, IgG index, cultures, and infectious markers
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal CSF with no pleocytosis, normal protein/glucose, absent oligoclonal bands, negative cytology and cultures — notably normal CSF which occurs in 5-10% of MS cases

optimal_actions.action: Order MRI of cervical and thoracic spine to evaluate for additional demyelinating lesions
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Short-segment cervical cord lesion at C3-C4, supporting dissemination in space

optimal_actions.action: Request MR spectroscopy of the dominant posterior fossa lesion to differentiate demyelination from neoplasm
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Spectroscopic pattern favoring demyelination over high-grade glioma (moderate choline, no lipid, elevated myoinositol)

optimal_actions.action: Request aquaporin-4 and MOG antibody testing to exclude NMOSD and MOGAD
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Both antibodies negative, excluding NMOSD and MOGAD

optimal_actions.action: Initiate IV methylprednisolone pulse therapy (1g/day for 5 days) as diagnostic and therapeutic trial for suspected tumefactive demyelination
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Interaction with diabetes medications requiring insulin sliding scale during steroid therapy

optimal_actions.action: Obtain follow-up MRI after steroid therapy to assess treatment response
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Dramatic reduction in lesion size and enhancement confirming tumefactive demyelination

optimal_actions.action: Request visual evoked potentials to assess for subclinical optic nerve involvement
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Prolonged P100 latency in right eye suggesting prior subclinical optic neuritis, providing evidence of dissemination in space

optimal_actions.action: Search literature on tumefactive MS and perioperative presentation to inform clinical decision-making
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Literature supporting perioperative unmasking of MS and steroid trial as diagnostic approach

critical_actions: Obtain urgent brain MRI with contrast to characterize the lesion causing acute quadriplegia and altered consciousness
Perform lumbar puncture with CSF cytology and flow cytometry to exclude CNS malignancy
Recognize the open ring enhancement pattern as suggestive of tumefactive demyelination rather than neoplasm
Initiate IV methylprednisolone pulse therapy as a diagnostic/therapeutic trial before proceeding to brain biopsy
Check drug interactions between high-dose corticosteroids and the patient's diabetes medications
Order MRI spine to evaluate for dissemination in space supporting MS diagnosis
Request NMO/MOG antibodies to exclude antibody-mediated conditions
contraindicated_actions: Proceeding directly to open brain biopsy without first trialing corticosteroid therapy — biopsy carries significant risk for a posterior fossa lesion and may be unnecessary
Administering thrombolytics (tPA) — patient is postoperative and the presentation is not consistent with acute ischemic stroke in a vascular territory
Starting empiric antibiotics for brain abscess without supporting evidence (patient is afebrile, CSF cultures negative)
Initiating chemotherapy or radiation without histological confirmation
Delaying neuroimaging in a patient with acute-onset severe neurological deficit
Abruptly discontinuing diabetes medications without providing alternative glycemic management during steroid therapy
key_reasoning_points: The acute onset of severe neurological deficits (quadriplegia, altered consciousness, aphasia) in the postoperative period initially suggests a perioperative vascular event but the MRI pattern does not match a vascular territory distribution
The large ring-enhancing posterior fossa lesion with mass effect mimics a brain tumor (especially glioblastoma), which is the most common initial misdiagnosis in tumefactive MS

The open (incomplete) ring enhancement pattern is a key distinguishing feature that favors demyelination over neoplasm — in demyelination, enhancement typically occurs at the leading edge of the lesion
Entirely normal CSF (including absent oligoclonal bands) occurs in approximately 5-10% of MS cases and should not exclude the diagnosis, particularly in the tumefactive variant
MR spectroscopy showing moderate choline elevation without marked lipid peak and elevated myoinositol supports demyelination over high-grade glioma

The prior episodes (transient leg numbness 2 years ago, transient blurry vision 8 months ago) represent likely prior demyelinating events that were misattributed, providing clinical evidence of dissemination in time
Dramatic clinical and radiographic response to IV methylprednisolone pulse therapy is a hallmark of tumefactive demyelination and helps distinguish it from neoplasm
Negative AQP4 and MOG antibodies help exclude NMOSD and MOGAD as alternative diagnoses
Surgical stress and general anesthesia are recognized triggers for acute MS relapses, explaining the perioperative onset
The cervical cord lesion found on spine MRI and prolonged VEP provide additional evidence of dissemination in space needed for MS diagnosis

metadata.difficulty_description: Diagnostic puzzle: acute postoperative presentation mimics stroke or brain tumor. The large ring-enhancing posterior fossa lesion strongly suggests glioblastoma. CSF is entirely normal (no oligoclonal bands), removing a typical MS diagnostic clue. Prior demyelinating episodes were misattributed to other conditions. Diagnosis requires recognizing the open ring enhancement pattern, obtaining MR spectroscopy, and observing dramatic steroid response.
